source.documentName: City of Cerritos Combined Financial Program FY 2025-2026
source.pageReference: pp. 69-87, Revenue Summary pp. 70-71, General Fund Revenue Detail pp. 73-80

categories.name: Sales Tax
categories.tldr: The city's #1 revenue source at $43.8M — nearly half of all General Fund revenue. For every dollar you spend in Cerritos, one penny comes back to the city. The Cerritos Auto Square generates about one-third of all sales tax.

lineItems.name: Cerritos Auto Square Sales Tax
lineItems.description: Sales tax from one of the largest auto malls in the world. Auto sales generate approximately one-third of the city's total sales tax revenue. However, high vehicle prices, elevated interest rates, and tightening consumer spending have caused a slowdown.
lineItems.tldr: The Auto Square is Cerritos's economic engine — one of the biggest auto malls in the world. But car prices are slowing sales down.

lineItems.name: Retail, Restaurant & Other Sales Tax
lineItems.description: Sales tax from Los Cerritos Center mall, Cerritos Towne Center retail, restaurants, and all other taxable purchases within city limits. The 9.75% sales tax rate includes 1% that goes directly to the city.
lineItems.tldr: Shopping, dining, and everyday purchases — when you shop in Cerritos, $0.01 of every dollar funds city services.

categories.name: Use of Money & Property
categories.tldr: Cerritos's second-largest revenue source at $21.8M — including $11.2M from ground leases at the Towne Center, Lincoln Station, Auto Square dealerships, and other city-owned properties, plus investment income.

lineItems.name: Ground Lease Portfolio
lineItems.description: Revenue from the city's ground lease portfolio projected to yield $11.2M in FY 2025-26. Properties include Cerritos Towne Center ($7.6M), Lincoln Station ($544K), Auto Square dealerships (Kia, Jaguar/Land Rover), Moore Street, and NW Studebaker/183.
lineItems.tldr: Cerritos owns land under the Towne Center and other properties, leasing it for $11.2M/year — a smart long-term investment that diversifies revenue.

lineItems.name: Investment & Interest Income
lineItems.description: Interest earned on the city's invested reserves and fund balances. The General Fund maintains reserves of approximately 35% of annual expenditures, consistent with the city's Financial Reserve Policy.
lineItems.tldr: Money the city earns by investing its savings wisely — like a savings account for Cerritos's rainy day fund.

lineItems.name: Facility Rentals & Other Property Revenue
lineItems.description: Revenue from rental participation agreements (Sheraton, AT&T), facility rentals, concessions (including Iron-Wood Nine Golf Course), and Community Facilities District revenue.
lineItems.tldr: Income from renting city facilities, the golf course, hotel partnerships, and other property.

categories.name: Other Taxes
categories.tldr: Business licenses, property tax (No-Low), hotel tax (TOT), CRA pass-through, and documentary transfer taxes — $12.7M that diversifies the city's revenue base.

lineItems.name: Property Tax (No-Low)
lineItems.description: Cerritos receives a relatively small share of property tax due to California's Prop 13 and the city's former redevelopment agency structure. The 'No-Low' property tax is the city's share after redevelopment pass-through allocations.
lineItems.tldr: A smaller revenue source than you'd think — California's Prop 13 limits how much cities get from property tax.

lineItems.name: Business License Tax
lineItems.description: Annual business license fees paid by businesses operating within Cerritos city limits.
lineItems.tldr: Every business in Cerritos pays a license fee — funding the services that make Cerritos a great place to do business.

lineItems.name: Transient Occupancy Tax (Hotel Tax)
lineItems.description: Tax collected from hotel guests staying in Cerritos, primarily from the Sheraton and other lodging establishments.
lineItems.tldr: Tax on hotel stays — visitors contribute to city services when they stay in Cerritos.

lineItems.name: Former CRA Pass-Through & Residual
lineItems.description: Revenue from the Successor Agency to the former Cerritos Redevelopment Agency, including pass-through payments and ABX1 26 property tax residual distributions.
lineItems.tldr: Leftover revenue from the old Redevelopment Agency — the state dissolved all city redevelopment agencies in 2012.

lineItems.name: General Tax Levy & Documentary Transfer
lineItems.description: General property tax levy and documentary transfer tax on real estate sales.
lineItems.tldr: Property-related taxes collected when homes are sold and from the general property tax levy.

categories.name: Current Fees & Services
categories.tldr: User fees for specific city services — building inspections, recreation program fees, golf course revenue, and service charges. Growing to $10.7M as the city explores fee-based cost recovery.

lineItems.name: Recreation & Golf Course Fees
lineItems.description: Fees from recreation programs, facility rentals, swim center, and Iron-Wood Nine Golf Course. The golf course was the first facility in Cerritos to use recycled water (since 1978).
lineItems.tldr: Fees from rec programs, swim center, and the golf course — which pioneered recycled water use back in 1978!

lineItems.name: Building Permits & Development Fees
lineItems.description: Fees collected for building permits, plan checks, inspections, and development review services.
lineItems.tldr: Fees paid by builders and developers — new construction pays for the city's review and inspection services.

lineItems.name: Other Service Charges
lineItems.description: Miscellaneous service fees including fingerprinting, gas/fuel sales, Cal-Card rebates, CCPA handling fees, and coffee/beverage sales.
lineItems.tldr: Various small fees — from fingerprinting to CCPA ticket handling charges.

categories.name: Intergovernmental Revenue
categories.tldr: Money from the state and federal government — mostly Motor Vehicle In-Lieu fees ($7.4M) that California distributes to cities based on population. Also includes SB 90 mandate reimbursements.

lineItems.name: Motor Vehicle In-Lieu Fee
lineItems.description: State-distributed revenue that replaced the former vehicle license fee. Distributed to cities based on population — a major and stable revenue source.
lineItems.tldr: When Californians pay car registration fees, the state sends a share back to cities based on population. Cerritos gets $7.4M.

lineItems.name: Grants, SB 90 & Other State Revenue
lineItems.description: Library grants, state mandate reimbursements (SB 90), and miscellaneous state/federal funding. Includes reimbursement for staff time and other agency contributions.
lineItems.tldr: State grants for the library, reimbursements for state-mandated programs, and other state/federal funding.

categories.name: Franchises, Licenses & Fines
categories.tldr: Franchise fees from utilities (Edison, SoCalGas, cable), building permits, parking fines, and court fines — smaller but important revenue streams totaling $4.5M.

lineItems.name: Utility & Cable Franchise Fees
lineItems.description: Franchise fees from Southern California Edison (electric), SoCalGas (gas), and cable TV providers for the right to operate within Cerritos city limits.
lineItems.tldr: Edison, SoCalGas, and cable companies pay the city for the right to operate here — funding city services.

lineItems.name: Licenses & Permits
lineItems.description: Building permits (city and county processing), excavation permits, green building permits, tree trimming permits, and occupancy permits.
lineItems.tldr: Permits for building, tree trimming, and excavation — making sure all work in Cerritos meets safety standards.

lineItems.name: Parking & Court Fines
lineItems.description: Revenue from parking citations, vehicle fines, administrative citations, and court fine distributions.
lineItems.tldr: Parking tickets and other fines — discouraging bad behavior while funding city services.

categories.name: Miscellaneous Revenue
categories.tldr: Small but notable: insurance reimbursements, unclaimed property, and other one-time revenue — less than 1% of the total but every dollar counts.

lineItems.name: Insurance, Unclaimed Property & Other
lineItems.description: Insurance reimbursements, sale of surplus property, unclaimed funds, and other miscellaneous revenue.
lineItems.tldr: Small amounts from insurance claims, selling old equipment, and other one-time sources.
